channel.title: Comments for page "Hector and Jacob"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Hector and Jacob"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
leaves along time for things to kind of fade and become distant through reflection, hence Hector's coldness

This kind of gets to my emerging issue with the portrayal of robots/AI in this canon: that they're too human, or at least assumed to be. I feel like when you take something that is, at least practically, indistinguishable from a human, then give it perfect recall, or the ability to tell the future, or a complete absence of aging, you don't just get a human plus those things, but an entirely different experience.

To steal a line from Neil Postman, if you take an ecosystem and add caterpillars to it, you don't get that ecosystem with caterpillars; you get a new ecosystem.

The AI's are presumably intended to evoke empathy through their resemblance to humans (and they do it quite well), but I can't help but feel that there's a lot of territory that could still be explored regarding what it is to be alive without falling back into the trope of anthropocentrism.

As for the tale itself, it's fine. I feel like so much has happened between reading the original tales and now that it's hard to feel all that much of a connection with the bot-soul. No voting for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough.

I'd argue that on top of being a robot, Hector's also been isolated from that family for the better part of two and half (or so) decades by the time this tale roles around, which leaves along time for things to kind of fade and become distant through reflection, hence Hector's coldness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hm. I really like a lot of the back and forth, but I just feel like it didn't really sell the emotional aspect to me. Maybe it's intentional because robot but Hector just came off as too cold for me to really feel like he cared about the family he lost. Conwell has a little more color but his behavior feels a little incongruous with his surroundings. There is some nice imagery here, though, making good use of the world you've crafted with AR.

Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad it turned around for you.

Even though I really like that line in question… >_>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I haven't read any of the other Anderson Robotics tales, but this still managed to be bittersweet and sad. The length was also perfect for achieving that, and I'm impressed that something so emotional could be done in such a short tale.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I instinctively downvoted at "something would certainly find him and utterly wreck his shit", which felt jarringly inappropriate for the context. But I'm a sucker for a well-delivered theme of regret and forgiveness, so that vote had turned around by the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really liked this. Felt contemplative and peaceful, and you managed to strike an interesting balance between heartwarming and creepy. There's something very intrinsically beautiful about the machine made for war relenting after realizing the futility of vengeance - even when the possibility of achieving that vengeance was conceived by chance. It doesn't overstay its welcome, and it manages to build both characters to the point just where you can end it on something of a bittersweet note. I couldn't find any reasonably awkward structural flaws or grammatical issues; at the end of the day, if this is an epilogue, it feels like a very fitting one.

item.title: I think this might be the shortest tale I've written...
item.content:encoded:
Alright, so I must be in an epilogue kind of mood, because, since SCP-3560 is now a thing, this serves as a bit of closure on the relationship Researcher Conwell and SCP-1360 had in the Peregrine series of tales.

Its short, but I like the characterization the previously muted SCP-1360 gets here.

Special thanks to the following for their critiques: Taffeta, salvagebar, djkaktus, Modern_Erasmus, minmin, DrBleep, LordStonefish

Enjoy the tale.

JC
